scope: design tranche CP0-CP7 with bounded canonical artifacts; no implementation of detector or templates yet, no contract flip or routing changes

checkpoints.title: scope lock
scope_in: Detection of runtime family for codebases without trustworthy upstream Dockerfiles
Templated Dockerfile generation per detected family (first wave: python, node, go)
Proof ladder (proposed -> build_proven -> runtime_proven) with explicit refusal lane
scope_out: Existing-build lane (already covered by ecosystem build contract / first-party PoC)
Lifecycle dispatch wiring, chart integration, contract flip on any specific ecosystem

checkpoints.title: existing vs generated contract truth boundary

decision_summary.existing_build_contract: Repo ships a Dockerfile (or skaffold.yaml / package.json#docker scripts) that AMOF treats as source of truth. Wires it; does not modify it.
decision_summary.generated_build_contract: Repo does not ship a trustworthy upstream build instruction. AMOF detects, templates, generates, and must run through the proof ladder.
decision_summary.lane_selector: When AMOF is asked to build a repo: existing wins if present and trustworthy; otherwise generated lane is entered. The lanes never silently merge; capability surface always names which lane produced the build.

checkpoints.title: runtime taxonomy + first-wave families
checkpoints.rationale: Each first-wave family has one dominant manifest, a clean distroless-or-slim runtime base, and a small per-language template surface. Deferred families either fan out across multiple manifests, have a heavy SDK/runtime split, or require per-service decomposition before they can be templated truthfully.

checkpoints.title: detection signals + confidence scoring

static: index.html at repo root with no other family manifest

checkpoints.soft_signal_role: Used only to break ties or pick an entrypoint; never to override hard signals.
checkpoints.ambiguity_handling: Hard signals from two or more in-wave families => status: refused, refusal_reason: polyglot_repo_no_per_service_map. Hard signal from a deferred family => status: refused, refusal_reason: runtime_family_not_in_first_wave.

checkpoints.title: machine-readable schema + examples
checkpoints.examples_validation_result: 4/4 examples validate against the schema (jsonschema verified locally)

checkpoints.title: first-wave Dockerfile templates

assumptions: Top-level requirements.txt or pyproject.toml describes deps
Application entrypoint is `app:app` or supplied via signal
known_risks: PyPI access required at build time unless deps are vendored
Native deps (e.g. cryptography, asyncpg) need build-essential layer
templates_named.proof_expectation: build_proven on host docker; runtime_proven via local docker run with port_open liveness on inferred_port

known_risks: npm registry access required unless deps are vendored
Postinstall scripts can pull native toolchain unexpectedly
templates_named.proof_expectation: build_proven on host docker; runtime_proven via local docker run with port_open liveness on inferred_port

assumptions: Module declares main package at repo root or under `cmd/<name>/`
known_risks: CGO_ENABLED=0 required for distroless/static; cgo deps would force runtime base swap to distroless/cc
Go module proxy access needed at build time unless vendored
templates_named.proof_expectation: build_proven on host docker; runtime_proven via local docker run with port_open liveness on inferred_port (default 8080)

checkpoints.why_partial: Template ids and shapes are pinned; the actual Dockerfile template files (under contracts/templates/ or similar) are intentionally not authored in this design tranche. They land in the first implementation slice (CP7-recommended).

checkpoints.title: proof model + false-confidence prevention

proof_ladder.amof_may_claim: We detected the runtime family with `accepted` confidence and rendered a Dockerfile from template <id@version>.
proof_ladder.amof_must_not_claim: That the image builds; that the image runs; that any health signal is observed; that the artifact is admissible by the lifecycle dispatcher.

proof_ladder.amof_may_claim: docker build exited 0; the image_digest is recorded; the build_in_substrate is named.
proof_ladder.amof_must_not_claim: That the image runs healthily; that the inferred entrypoint actually serves traffic; that any port is open.

proof_ladder.amof_may_claim: The image was started in the named substrate, the inferred entrypoint did not crash, and a minimal liveness signal (port_open / healthcheck_ok / log_pattern_seen) was observed within the proof timeout.
proof_ladder.amof_must_not_claim: Production readiness; persistence soundness; security posture; performance characteristics. These are separate downstream contracts.

proof_ladder.amof_may_claim: Why we refused (refusal_reason) and what the operator can do to advance.
proof_ladder.amof_must_not_claim: Anything about the codebase's buildability or runtime behavior.

false_confidence_prevention_rules: Status MUST NOT advance unless the proof for the new tier is materialized in the artifact (`build_proof` for build_proven; `build_proof` + `runtime_proof` for runtime_proven). Schema enforces via allOf/if/then.
UI/API surfaces MUST display the `status` value verbatim and MUST visually differentiate `proposed` from the proven tiers.
Lifecycle dispatcher MUST NOT admit a generated artifact as a deploy source unless `status >= build_proven`.
Operator-facing health labels MUST require `status == runtime_proven`.
Refusal artifacts MUST include `refusal_reason` and SHOULD include `risk_flags` so the operator sees the evidence.

checkpoints.title: first implementation tranche recommendation
what_to_implement: Add scripts/amof/generated_build/detector.py with one entry point: detect_runtime(repo_root: Path) -> dict matching the contract schema.
Implement only the python family path end-to-end. Other families return (kind: refused, refusal_reason: runtime_family_not_in_first_wave).
Implement the polyglot refusal: if both python hard signals and node hard signals are present, return refused.
Implement the existing-build pre-emption: if a Dockerfile exists at the repo root, return refused with refusal_reason: existing_build_contract_present.
Add focused unit tests against fixture trees (python repo, node repo, go repo, polyglot repo, repo with existing Dockerfile, empty repo).
Wire one small CLI entry: `python -m amof.generated_build detect <repo_path>` that prints the artifact JSON. Pure read-only.
what_to_explicitly_defer: Template rendering (no Dockerfile generation in this slice)
Build proof / runtime proof execution (no docker build run yet)
checkpoints.why_python_first: Python detection has the cleanest single-file hard signal (pyproject.toml or requirements.txt). The codebase under hand (AMOF itself) also uses Python, which gives a self-consistency check at zero cost (running the detector on repos/amof should not produce a generated contract, because the repo already ships Dockerfile.controlplane / Dockerfile.controlplane-base / Dockerfile.controlplane-deps -- the existing-build pre-emption path).

canonical_artifacts_created: contracts/INDEX.md (updated to list the new build-generated lane)

blockers.message: Template ids and shapes are pinned in this design slice but the actual Dockerfile template files are intentionally not authored. Land them in the first implementation slice.
